Architecture

Academic · Institutional Design · Studio 6 · 2021

IIAD — Dialogue : Space

A campus design for the International Institute of Art & Design, exploring the nature of engagement of programmes with spaces. The design proposes Architecture as a Dialogue — in a campus where exchange of knowledge is a constant process, the spaces become the instigators for collaboration and interaction across three departments: Art, Architecture and Fashion.

Institutional Design Campus Planning 10 Acres · 41,500 SQM Mixed-Use SketchUp · AutoCAD
LocationYelahanka, Bangalore
Year2021
TypeInstitutional · Campus
Site Area10 Acres (41,500 SQM)
StudioAD Studio 6 · Nitte SAPD

Concept — Dialogue : Space · Planning principles · Division of programmes · Hierarchy of spaces

3
Departments — Art, Architecture, Fashion
5
Floors in the Architecture block · 7,500 SQM
11
Studios with 11 theory classrooms
10
Acres · Yelahanka educational hub, Bangalore

Concept & Masterplan

Architecture as Dialogue

The masterplan is developed around three core principles: central block placement with peripheral vehicular circulation; division of spaces based on zones and separation of function; and provision of extra spill-out spaces while maintaining orientation. Buildings are placed centrally with only peripheral circulation to create alleyes — shaded informal corridors that become the hotspots for student interaction.

The entrance features three pockets of landscape designed as a buffer from the main road. The hostels are lined at the north of the site to act as a physical edge to the campus. Pedestrian circulation is the primary focus, with buildings arranged around alleyes to promote outside activities.

Planning

Spaces zoned to have at least one connection allowing constant interaction. Separates formal administration zones from informal learning and teaching zones.

Division of Programs

Buildings with clear department segregation transformed to combine different programmes — enabling design thinking and interdisciplinary exchange within the realm.

Hierarchy of Spaces

Design studios set open for interaction alongside common spaces. Each space allocated a set activity the student can transition into — classrooms, atria, studios.

Masterplan — 10 acres, Yelahanka · Alleys as design element · Form development diagrams

Architecture Block

Studio + Classroom Module

The architecture block is divided into two zones: formal administration and informal learning. The block has 5 floors with the auditorium located at 4th level. The studio + classroom module is designed to be a flexible space, changeable according to requirement — each floor has over 40 sqm of dialogue space for one-on-one discussions and reviews.

The structural layout follows a grid of 10m x 6m max with 300mm by 450mm rectangular column. An open terrace acts as a "construction yard" where all structural workshops can take place. The material library on the same floor ensures easy accessibility. Two blocks are connected via a bridge acting as a transition from formal to informal spaces.

Architecture block — ground, 4th and 5th floor plans · Schematic plans · Isometric view · Floor area: 1,500 SQM · 5 floors · 7,500 SQM total

Fashion Block

The Alley as Collaborative Zone

The Fashion department is located opposite to the Architecture department and adjacent to the Art block, with close proximity to indicate the similarities in their professional programme. The alley between the Art block and Fashion block becomes a collaborative zone — initiating a dialogue between the programmes.

The Fashion block sits above a podium — an indoor stadium for the campus — located below a green roof at G-2600mm. The atrium is designed as a ramp + hangout for fashion students to showcase their work. A bridge from the architecture block at +8000mm serves both circulation and display purposes.

Fashion block — ground, first and second floor plans · Schematic plans · Isometric view

Elevations & Sections

Facade as Programme

The alley facade between the Fashion and Art blocks is designed to represent each department through primary shapes — circle for art, square for architecture. The building uses circular perforations on the warm-toned facade, creating a distinctive campus streetscape. Sections reveal the stepped hierarchy achieving abundant natural lighting in all studios.

Elevation at North (Architecture), South (Fashion), West (Art) · Sections AA', BB' · Render of alley facade
